channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Iran War’s Persistent Threat to Farmers in Poor Countries
item.description: A reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would do little to swiftly ease the pain inflicted by higher prices for fertilizer, food and fuel in Ivory Coast.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman and Finbarr O’Reilly
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Markets Recoil in Global Sell-Off Driven by Tech Stocks
item.description: Chipmakers led the way down in South Korea, where the main index plunged 10.5 percent. The Nasdaq in the U.S. was down 2.2 percent at Tuesday’s open.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi, Jason Karaian and Aruni Soni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Traders in Seoul. The decline in South Korean stocks on Tuesday at one point triggered a 20-minute trading halt by the exchange operator.

item.title: Brexit Has Cost the UK Growth, Analysts Say, in the Decade Since the Vote
item.description: Citing lower trade and investment, analysts broadly agree that Britain’s economy is smaller than it would have been if the country had stayed in the E.U.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ten years ago, British voters approved by a slim margin a proposal for the country to leave the European Union. The economic results, economists say, have not been glowing.

item.title: ‘A Terrible Inheritance’: Could Andy Burnham Succeed Where Starmer Failed?
item.description: The likely successor to Prime Minister Keir Starmer will inherit the same challenges of economic stagnation and ascendant populism. Will a divided nation be prepared to give him time?
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham celebrating after his swearing-in in Parliament on Monday.

item.title: The Extraordinary Embezzlement Case That Rocked Scottish Politics
item.description: Peter Murrell, the husband of the former Scottish leader Nicola Sturgeon, was sentenced on Tuesday after he admitted to buying a bizarre range of items with the Scottish National Party’s money.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Jane Barlow/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Peter Murrell, center, arriving at Edinburgh High Court in May. He was sentenced on Tuesday to more than five years in prison.

item.title: Supreme Court Bars Lawsuit After Prison Guards Shaved Inmate’s Dreadlocks
item.description: Damon Landor, a Rastafarian, tried to sue Louisiana prison officials for violating his religious rights.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Damon Landor sought to sue state prison officials in Louisiana for forcibly shaving his head.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Claiming Cisco Systems Helped China Target Falun Gong
item.description: The court’s decision could have broader implications for whether companies can be held liable for aiding in international human rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Falun Gong followers during a silent protest outside the Communist Party’s headquarters in Beijing in 1999.

item.title: Looking Back on the U.S.A.’s Many Founders, 250 Years In
item.description: In the past 50 years, the way we tell the story of the Revolution has become dramatically more complex. Can it still inspire us all?
item.dc:creator: Jane Kamensky
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Universal History Archive/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The five-man drafting committee presenting the Declaration of Independence to Congress, in an early 19th-century painting by John Trumbull.

item.title: Trump Gives Teamsters a Chance to Shed Oversight Meant to Curb Mob Ties
item.description: Sean M. O’Brien, re-elected to a second term leading the union, has used a relationship with President Trump to end court-ordered corruption monitoring.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sean M. O’Brien, the Teamsters president, was overwhelming re-elected to his second term at the union’s convention last week.

item.title: Photos Behind Kennedy Center Tarps Show No Sign of Trump’s Name
item.description: Images circulated by an activist group reveal bare marble where President Trump’s name once resided. The Kennedy Center previously told a federal judge it had been removed.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: In a sworn declaration this month, the Kennedy Center said it had removed the words “The Donald J. Trump and” from its facade.

item.title: Trump on the Shabby Condition of the Reflecting Pool: Not My Fault
item.description: President Trump said the blooms of green algae and the peeling polyurethane had nothing to do with the rushed $16.4 million makeover he had ordered.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: Ongoing work on Sunday at the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, which has been troubled for decades by leaks and algae blooms that turn its water green.

item.title: Justice Dept. Withdraws Grand Jury Subpoenas of Journalists
item.description: The administration backed down after the news organizations, The Wall Street Journal and The Washington Post, fought them in sealed filings.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: The Justice Department had sought information related to reporting on top officials warning President Trump of the risks of a major military campaign against Iran and on U.S. military action in Venezuela.

item.title: Searching for Clues in Jeffrey Epstein’s Boyhood
item.description: “Jeff” grew up in an insular world, kept company with a few brainy boys and fixated on girls. Was a monster hiding in plain sight?
item.dc:creator: Lisa Miller and Steve Eder

item.title: The N.Y. Primaries Will Answer Key Questions: Mamdani, Israel and More
item.description: The congressional primaries in New York on Tuesday will answer key questions about the potency of super PACs and the extent of Zohran Mamdani’s influence.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Zohran Mamdani won the New York City mayoralty last year with more votes than any candidate in decades.

item.title: Europe Is Sweltering in an Extreme Heat Wave. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The most intense conditions are happening in Britain, France and Spain, which are all under high-level heat warnings.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: 40 People Drown in France Amid Scorching Temperatures
item.description: Most of the victims were young people swimming in unsupervised areas, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: People trying to find respite in the shade in Toulouse, France, on Monday.

item.title: Europe Created Heat-Wave Protections. Now Comes the ‘Crash Test.’
item.description: Searing temperatures in Western Europe are drawing comparisons to 2003, when a deadly heat wave sparked a reckoning.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: A visitor to the Palace of Versailles in France on Monday, where temperatures exceeded 104 degrees Fahrenheit.

item.title: CBS News Hired an Independent Watchdog. What’s He Doing?
item.description: With CBS News rocked by controversy, some viewers have turned to the network ombudsman for a public response. So far, they haven’t gotten one.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Michael Brochstein/SOPA Images, via LightRocket, via Getty Images
item.media:description: CBS News appointed Kenneth R. Weinstein, shown in 2024, as its ombudsman in September under an agreement with the Federal Communications Commission.

item.title: Ferraris and Shell Companies: Five Charged in Medicare Fraud Schemes
item.description: The Justice Department is taking aim at pricey “skin substitutes,” an overused wound care treatment that cost the government $10 billion in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff
item.media:description: Mehmet Oz, center, the head of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, with Todd Blanche, right, the acting attorney general, and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. during a briefing at the Justice Department on Tuesday.

item.title: Medicare Bleeds Billions on Pricey Bandages, and Doctors Get a Cut
item.description: Medicare spending on “skin substitutes” made of dried placenta has soared as doctors pocket lucrative discounts from sellers.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Katie Thomas
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times

item.title: Social Security Is Running Out of Money, Fast
item.description: To save the program, we need to eliminate the payroll tax cap.
item.dc:creator: Bernie Moreno and Elizabeth Warren

item.title: Is This the Year Florida Turns Blue?
item.description: David Jolly must persuade voters who have been increasingly hostile to Democrats that he’s a different kind of Democrat.
item.dc:creator: Michael Grunwald

item.title: Bread and Roses
item.description: We look at the democratic socialists who are leading some of America’s big cities.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bernie Sanders and Zohran Mamdani at a rally last week.

item.title: As Chinese Tech Pulls Ahead, U.S. Fears It Will Become Dependent on It
item.description: Chinese firms have some of the world’s most advanced technology. But U.S. officials say relying on it could come with a downside.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
